revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q24870749]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yasoshima Festival''' (八十島祭, やそしままつり / やそじままつり / やそじまのまつり) was a ritual performed at {{ill|Naniwazu|ja|難波津|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwazu|qq=}} as part of the '''Imperial enthronement ceremonies''' from the '''Heian period''' to the '''Kamakura period'''. It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|qq=|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}
== See Also ==
* [[Ikukunitama Shrine]]

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Bibliography ==
* See original article for full references.

- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

<!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis-->
{{wikidata link|Q24870749}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|qq=draft|" to "|"
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q24870749]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yasoshima Festival''' (八十島祭, やそしままつり / やそじままつり / やそじまのまつり) was a ritual performed at {{ill|Naniwazu|ja|難波津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwazu|qq=}} as part of the '''Imperial enthronement ceremonies''' from the '''Heian period''' to the '''Kamakura period'''. It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

revision.comment: Appending wikidata link for Q24870749
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q24870749]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yasoshima Festival''' (八十島祭, やそしままつり / やそじままつり / やそじまのまつり) was a ritual performed at {{ill|Naniwazu|ja|難波津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwazu|qq=}} as part of the '''Imperial enthronement ceremonies''' from the '''Heian period''' to the '''Kamakura period'''. It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]" to ""
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q24870749]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yasoshima Festival''' (八十島祭, やそしままつり / やそじままつり / やそじまのまつり) was a ritual performed at {{ill|Naniwazu|ja|難波津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwazu|qq=}} as part of the '''Imperial enthronement ceremonies''' from the '''Heian period''' to the '''Kamakura period'''. It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}
== See Also ==
* [[Ikukunitama Shrine]]

revision.comment: Bot: add interwikis from Wikidata
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q24870749]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yasoshima Festival''' (八十島祭, やそしままつり / やそじままつり / やそじまのまつり) was a ritual performed at {{ill|Naniwazu|ja|難波津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwazu|qq=}} as part of the '''Imperial enthronement ceremonies''' from the '''Heian period''' to the '''Kamakura period'''. It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}
== See Also ==
* [[Ikukunitama Shrine]]

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}
== See Also ==
* [[Ikukunitama Shrine]]

It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}

A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]

The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. 2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24870749]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yasoshima Festival''' (八十島祭, やそしままつり / やそじままつり / やそじまのまつり) was a ritual performed at {{ill|Naniwazu|ja|難波津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwazu|qq=}} as part of the '''Imperial enthronement ceremonies''' from the '''Heian period''' to the '''Kamakura period'''. It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ([[:en:Osaka Prefecture|Osaka Prefecture]], [[:en:Osaka|Osaka City]])}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

* [[Ikukunitama Shrine]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:八十島祭]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q24870749]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Yasoshima Festival''' (八十島祭, やそしままつり / やそじままつり / やそじまのまつり) was a ritual performed at {{ill|Naniwazu|ja|難波津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwazu|qq=}} as part of the '''Imperial enthronement ceremonies''' from the '''Heian period''' to the '''Kamakura period'''. It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ({{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City}})}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}
== See Also ==
* [[Ikukunitama Shrine]]

== References ==
{{Reflist}}

== Bibliography ==
* See original article for full references.

It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

== Overview ==
The '''Yasoshima Festival''' was held at '''Naniwazu''' in the year following the '''Daijōsai (大嘗祭, Great Thanksgiving Festival)''' as part of the '''imperial accession rituals'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} The name '''"Yasoshima"''' is believed to refer to the entire '''Japanese archipelago''' (Ōyashima, 大八洲).{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}  

The earliest recorded instance of this festival took place in '''Kashō 3 (850)''', during the enthronement of '''Emperor Montoku''', while the '''last recorded event''' occurred in '''Gen'nin 1 (1224)''' for '''Emperor Go-Horikawa'''. A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

The '''ritual procedures''' are recorded in '''Volume 3 of the Engishiki (延喜式, "Procedures of the Engi Era")''' under the '''Yasoshima Kamisai (八十島神祭) section'''. Additionally, the sequence of events is described in detail in the '''Eke-shidai (江家次第)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

According to these records:
- A '''female court official (典侍, tenji)''', traditionally the '''imperial wet nurse''', was always appointed as the '''ritual envoy (祭使, saishi)'''.  
- She was accompanied by '''officials from the Jingi-kan (神祇官, Department of Divinities)''', '''Miko (御巫)''', and '''Ikushima Miko (生島巫)'''.  
- The delegation traveled to '''Naniwazu''', where the '''envoy opened a box containing the Emperor's garments''' and ritually '''shook it to the sound of a koto''' before finally '''casting offerings into the sea'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

There are various interpretations of the festival's '''purpose''', but given the participation of '''Ikushima Miko''', it is widely believed that the '''main deities were Ikushima-no-Kami (生島神) and Tarushima-no-Kami (足島神)''', deities enshrined within the '''Imperial Palace'''.<ref>See {{ill|List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|ja|宮中・京中の式内社一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha Shrines in the Imperial Court|1=List of Shikinaisha in the Imperial Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11453224}}.</ref>  

The dominant theory suggests that this ritual served to:
1. '''Deify the land''' through the worship of Ikushima and Tarushima.  
2. '''Infuse the Emperor’s clothing with the spirit of the land (shamanistic soul-invitation)'''.  
3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ({{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City}})}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}

It has since been discontinued.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

A total of '''22 Yasoshima Festivals''' have been documented.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}} One exception exists where the '''Yasoshima Festival was performed the year before the Daijōsai.'''{{Sfn|大和岩雄|2000}}

3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ({{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City|1=Osaka}})}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

3. '''Symbolically transfer the land’s divine authority to the Emperor''', thereby reinforcing his '''sovereignty over the land'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}  

Over time, the '''festival's nature changed''', and participation expanded to include '''the Empress's court (中宮使)''' and '''the Crown Prince's court (東宮使)'''. The '''procession''' became more extravagant, featuring '''elaborate displays and female attendants in luxurious attire'''. Notably, during '''Emperor Nijō's Yasoshima Festival''', '''Taira no Kiyomori's daughter''' served as the ritual envoy, forming a particularly grand procession.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}

[[File:Ikukunitama-jinja haiden-2.JPG|thumb|220px|right|{{center|[[Ikukunitama Shrine]] ({{ill|Osaka Prefecture|en|Osaka Prefecture|WD=Q122723|lt=Osaka Prefecture|1=Osaka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka City|1=Osaka}})}}{{small|A shrine dedicated to Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami.}}]]
The '''ritual site''' at '''Naniwazu''' is associated with '''Ikukunitama Shrine (生國魂神社)''', which enshrines '''Ikushima-no-Kami and Tarushima-no-Kami''', suggesting a historical link between the two. The '''Engishiki''' further notes that '''offerings''' were made to the following local deities during the festival:
- '''Sumiyoshi Kami (住吉神, at Sumiyoshi Taisha)'''
- '''Ōyorokoro Kami (大依羅神, at Ōyorokoro Shrine)'''
- '''Kaijin (海神, "Sea Gods", unknown)'''
- '''Tarumi Kami (垂水神, at Tarumi Shrine)'''
- '''Suminomichi Kami (住道神, at Nakatomi Sumuchi Shrine)'''{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Over time, the '''influence of Sumiyoshi Taisha grew''', and by '''Chōryaku 1 (1037)''', records indicate that '''Sumiyoshi Shrine officials successfully moved the festival site from "Kumakawa-jiri" to their own "Taiya-hama"'''. However, the original location of '''Kumakawa-jiri''' remains unknown.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第2巻|1988|pp=39-44}}  

Archaeological findings from the '''Gotanjima Ruins (吹田市南吹田, Suita City, Minami-Suita)''' suggest a connection to the festival. Many '''ritual artifacts''', such as '''bronze mirrors and iron arrowheads''', have been unearthed from an ancient riverbed, but the '''absence of dolls or bronze bells''', as well as the fact that the site is '''inland rather than coastal''', has led some scholars to '''question the connection'''.<ref>*Ancient Port or Ritual Site? – Unraveling the Mystery of the Gotanjima Ruins*, Suita City Museum, 2016, pp. 38-39.</ref>

It is also known that many '''emperors prior to Emperor Kōnin (光仁天皇)''' visited '''Naniwa the year after their Daijōsai''', suggesting that the '''Yasoshima Festival may date back at least to the 7th century'''.{{Sfn|八十島祭（国史）}}{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}} Some scholars even theorize that its origins may reach back to the '''5th century, during the Kawachi Dynasty (河内王朝)''', or that it was connected to '''Naniwa Palace (難波宮)'''.{{Sfn|新修大阪市史 本文編 第1巻|1988|pp=595-599}}{{Sfn|岡田精司|2011|pp=189-192}}
